does: This paper investigates hallucination detection and mitigation in the Whisper speech recognition model by analyzing its internal representations. The authors extract activations from Whisper's audio encoder and examine both raw activations and Sparse AutoEncoder (SAE) latents, demonstrating that hallucination-related information is linearly separable within these representation spaces. They identify that discriminative features are concentrated in sparse subsets and become more prominent in deeper encoder layers. The paper proposes two steering strategies—activation-space steering and SAE latent-space steering—that intervene on model representations at inference time. SAE-based steering achieves substantial improvements, reducing hallucination rates from 72.63% to 14.11% for Whisper small and from 86.88% to 27.33% for Whisper large-v3 on non-speech audio, while maintaining competitive performance on speech data with minimal word error rate degradation.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in mechanistic interpretability and representation steering for large language and multimodal models. It extends prior research on using Sparse AutoEncoders to decompose model representations and identify interpretable features, methodologies developed in the context of understanding transformer internals. The paper also connects to a growing body of work on hallucination detection and mitigation in neural models, including approaches based on internal representations and probing classifiers. The steering approach is informed by techniques like activation patching and representation intervention used to understand and control model behavior without full retraining. This research directly addresses known failures of Whisper, a widely-deployed model, by applying interpretability tools to a practical problem in automatic speech recognition.
enables: This work opens avenues for understanding and controlling hallucinations in other autoregressive audio and multimodal models through representation steering. The success of SAE-based steering suggests that sparse decompositions of model internals could enable efficient, post-hoc control mechanisms that don't require expensive fine-tuning. The approach potentially generalizes to other failure modes in speech and vision models where internal representations encode information about input validity or output reliability. The paper demonstrates that hallucination-related features become increasingly linearly separable in deeper layers, which could inform better architectural designs or training objectives for robust speech recognition. Future work might extend this framework to detect and mitigate other types of model failures, integrate steering into deployment pipelines, or investigate whether similar sparse feature structures exist for hallucinations in language and multimodal models.
